content.subtitle: Use
items: General or scientific facts and definitions: If you **have** faith in something, you **believe** in something you cannot prove.

content.subtitle: Form
content.formula: if + a present tense, will + bare infinitive

content.subtitle: Use
items: Real or likely conditions in the present or future and their results in the present and future: If you **have** a birthday party, you'**ll get** loads of cool presents!
: If you'**re working** till half past six, we'**ll have** dinner at about eight.
: If you **have revised** properly, you **won't have** any problems in the test next week.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: We can also use may, might, can, could, shall, should, ought to, have to instead of will, depending on the meaning. ✓ If you **have** a birthday party, you **might get** loads of cool presents!

We can also use should + bare infinitive instead of present simple. This suggests the situation is possible, but unlikely to happen. ✓ If you **should bump** into Alex at the concert, you'll be able to get a lift home.

We can also use a form of the first conditional to give instructions about real or likely situations in the present or future. This is: if + a present tense, imperative. ✓ If you **decide** to have a birthday party, **tell** me!

sections.title: Unless, in case, as/so long as, provided (that)

content.subtitle: Meanings and examples
rows: I'll be there at six **unless** I get delayed. (= except if I get delayed / if I don’t get delayed)
Let’s take our wellies **in case** it’s muddy. (= because it might be muddy)
**As long as** I’m happy, my parents don’t care what job I do. / **So long as** I’m happy, my parents don’t care what job I do.
**Provided (that)** I’m happy, my parents don’t care what job I do.

content.subtitle: Form
content.formula: if + past simple or past continuous, would + bare infinitive

content.subtitle: Use
items: Impossible, unlikely or hypothetical conditions in the present or future and their results in the present or future: If you **had** a beard, you **would look** just like Charles Dickens! / If you **were flying** to Rio, **would** you **get** there much quicker?
Advice: If I **were** you, I would think very carefully about my future. (more formal) / If I **was** you, I'd have a party at the weekend! (more informal)

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: We can also use *might* or *could* instead of *would*, depending on the meaning. (Note: *could* here often means *would be able to*.) ✓ If we **were** older, we **could go** on holiday on our own.
We can also use *could* in the *if* clause. Here, it means *was/were able to*. ✓ If I **could** drive, I'd buy a car.

content.subtitle: Form
content.formula: if + past perfect (simple or continuous), would + have + past participle

content.subtitle: Use
items: Hypothetical conditions in the past and their results in the past: If you **had worn** a fake beard, no one **would have known** who you were! (= You didn't wear a fake beard so people knew who you were.) / If she'**d been wearing** her new glasses, I **would have noticed** them. (= She wasn't wearing her new glasses so I didn't notice them.)

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: We can also use *might*, *could* or *should* instead of *would*, depending on the meaning. ✓ If I **had done** some revision, I **might / could / should** have passed the exam.

content.subtitle: Form 1
content.formula: if + past perfect (simple or continuous), would + bare infinitive

content.subtitle: Use
items: Hypothetical past condition and a present result: If I **had listened** to my parents, I **wouldn't be** in so much trouble now. (= I didn't listen to my parents so I'm in lots of trouble now.)

content.subtitle: Form 2
content.formula: if + past simple or past continuous, would + have + past participle

content.subtitle: Use
items: Hypothetical present condition and a past result: If I **had** a mobile, I **would have called** you last night. (= I don't have a mobile so I didn't call you last night.)

content.subtitle: Form
items: Should I/you/he/etc … instead of If I/you/he/etc should …
Were I/you/he/etc … instead of If I/you/he/etc were …
Had I/you/he/etc … instead of If I/you/he/etc had …

content.subtitle: Use
items: More formal form of the first conditional (with should): **Should** the situation worsen, the United Nations is prepared to send in a peacekeeping force. (= If the situation should worsen…)
More formal form of the second conditional: **Were** the situation to worsen, the United Nations would be prepared to send in a peacekeeping force. (= If the situation were to worsen…)
More formal form of the third conditional: **Had** the situation worsened, the United Nations would have been prepared to send in a peacekeeping force. (= If the situation had worsened…)

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: For all conditional sentences (first, second, third, mixed, inverted), when the condition comes before the result it is usually followed by a comma. When the result comes first, no comma is necessary. ✓ If I had a mobile, I would have called you last night. ✓ I would have called you last night if I had a mobile.
In conditional sentences, modals (will, would, could, etc) are sometimes followed by a continuous infinitive. ✓ We’d still **be waiting** if you hadn’t turned up.

content.subtitle: American English note
content.description: With second and third conditionals in informal conversation, speakers of American English sometimes use would or would have in the if clause. This is very unusual in British English.
examples: US: How would you feel if this **happened / would happen** to you? UK: How would you feel if this **happened** to you?
US: I would have felt awful if that **had happened / would have happened** to me. UK: I would have felt awful if that **had happened** to me.

sections.title: A: Understanding conditionals
sections.instructions: Read each conditional sentence. Then answer the question by choosing YES or NO.

items.context: If Carl doesn't come to the party, I'll be really upset.
items.sentence: Is it possible that Carl won't come to the party?

items.context: If my neighbour became prime minister, he'd give everyone a million euros.
items.sentence: Is it likely that the neighbour will become prime minister?

items.context: If my neighbour became prime minister, he'd give everyone a million euros.
items.sentence: Is it possible that the neighbour will become prime minister?

items.context: If Helen weren't here, I don't know what we'd do.
items.sentence: Is Helen here now?

items.context: If Tarek had had enough money, he'd have bought a car.
items.sentence: Did Tarek have enough money?

items.context: If Tarek had had enough money, he'd have bought a car.
items.sentence: Did he buy a car?

items.context: If Debbie hadn't gone to the UK, she wouldn't have met Will.
items.sentence: Did Debbie go to the UK?

items.context: If Debbie hadn't gone to the UK, she wouldn't have met Will.
items.sentence: Did she meet Will?

items.context: If the police had investigated the case thoroughly, they wouldn't have arrested Keren.
items.sentence: Did the police investigate the case thoroughly?

items.context: If the police had investigated the case thoroughly, they wouldn't have arrested Keren.
items.sentence: Did they arrest Keren?

items.context: Had she not gone to university, she might not have started her own business.
items.sentence: Did she go to university?

items.context: Had she not gone to university, she might not have started her own business.
items.sentence: Did she start her own business?

sections.title: B: Complete the definitions
sections.instructions: Match each sentence beginning (1–8) with the correct ending (A–H).

items.sentence: If you bully someone, **A** you don't treat them with respect.
items.answer: A – you don't treat them with respect.

items.sentence: If you accuse someone, **B** you say they have done something wrong.
items.answer: B – you say they have done something wrong.

items.sentence: If you are a liar, **C** you don't tell the truth.
items.answer: C – you don't tell the truth.

items.sentence: If you are rich, **D** you have lots of money.
items.answer: D – you have lots of money.

items.sentence: If you are lucky, **E** good things happen to you.
items.answer: E – good things happen to you.

items.sentence: If you are lazy, **F** you don't work hard.
If you look up to someone, **G** you respect them.
If you look down on someone, **H** you don't respect them.

sections.title: C: First conditional
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verbs in the box.

items.sentence: If she ...... at five o'clock, she'll be there by half past seven.
We ...... you if we have any problems.
It might not be a good idea to go out tonight if you ...... an important test in the morning.
If you ...... ill all day, you shouldn't come to the club tonight.
If you should ...... my wallet, call me on my mobile immediately!
Let's get a different DVD if you ...... that one already.
If Sean ...... so hard lately, he'll welcome the chance to have a few days off.
If you're going into town, ...... a video for tonight while you're there!
If you see Carol tonight, ...... to say hello from me!
Don't feel you have to come if you ...... to.

sections.title: D: Unless, in case, as long as, provided
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: This time in six months I'll be at university **unless / if** I decide to take a year off first.
We'd better leave early tomorrow **unless / in case** there's a lot of traffic when we get to Doncaster.
You can watch the film **as long as / in case** you promise to go straight to bed when it finishes.
Let's go to Mirabella's tonight **unless / if** you haven't been there before.
**Unless / So long as** I've still got my health, I don't mind how poor I am!
You'd better take a sweater with you now **if / in case** it gets cold tonight.
Do what you like **provided / unless** you don't make any noise.
I'm not going to worry **unless / as long as** she hasn't called by midnight.

sections.title: E: Second conditional
sections.instructions: Write the complete second conditional sentence.

items.context: I don't live alone so I don't get lonely.
items.answer: If I lived alone, I'd get lonely.

items.context: Don's got such a lot of homework that he's not going to play football tonight.
items.answer: If Don didn't have so much homework, he'd play football tonight.

items.context: I can't swim so I'm not going scuba diving with Terry.
items.answer: If I could swim, I'd go scuba diving with Terry.

items.context: We're not going to order a pizza because we don't have enough money.
items.answer: We'd order pizza if we had enough money.

items.context: We're not staying in the same hotel, so we can't share a room.
items.answer: If we were staying in the same hotel, we could share a room.

items.context: I'm not you, but I think you should call Antony right now!
items.answer: If I were you, I'd call Antony right now.

items.context: I can't come because I have to help my dad with something.
items.answer: I'd come if I didn't have to help my dad with something.

items.context: I don't go to bed as late as you so I'm able to get up early in the morning.
items.answer: If I went to bed as late as you, I wouldn't be able to get up early in the morning.

sections.title: F: Third conditional
sections.instructions: Match each sentence beginning (1–9) with the correct ending (A–I).

items.sentence: If I'd started learning English a year earlier, **A** I would have done this book last year!
items.answer: A – I would have done this book last year!

items.sentence: If a meteor hadn't struck the Earth, **B** the dinosaurs wouldn't have become extinct.
items.answer: B – the dinosaurs wouldn't have become extinct.

items.sentence: If my best friend had won the lottery last summer, **C** he/she would have moved to a bigger house.
items.answer: C – he/she would have moved to a bigger house.

items.sentence: If my parents hadn't met, **D** I wouldn't have been born.
items.answer: D – I wouldn't have been born.

items.sentence: Everyone on board the Titanic would have survived **E** if there had been enough lifeboats.
items.answer: E – if there had been enough lifeboats.

items.sentence: Van Gogh wouldn't have died in poverty **F** if he had been recognised as a great painter during his lifetime.
items.answer: F – if he had been recognised as a great painter during his lifetime.

items.sentence: Madonna wouldn't have made any records **G** if she hadn't been discovered.
items.answer: G – if she hadn't been discovered.

items.sentence: If George Bush hadn't become President of the USA in January 2001, **H** lots of things would have been different.
items.answer: H – lots of things would have been different.

items.sentence: If Greece hadn't won the European Cup in July 2004, **I** I would have been very upset.
items.answer: I – I would have been very upset.

sections.title: G: Multiple choice (Conditionals)
sections.passage: If this theory about parallel universes (1) ...... correct, it (2) ...... that we're living in more than one universe at the same time. But if that (3) ...... true - if I was doing different things in different universes - (4) ...... about it. And if I (5) ...... about it, how could it be me doing these things? If I (6) ...... the lottery last year in a parallel universe, and decided to sail round the world, how is that 'me'? If I do something, it (7) ...... consequences. For example, if I (8) ...... study, I might not pass my exams. If I just (9) ...... about the universe I actually live in, then I'm sure (10) ...... okay!

sections.title: H: Mixed conditionals
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verbs in brackets.

items.sentence: If (you / do) ...... your homework last night, you'd know the answer to this question!
We (not / stand) ...... by the side of the road at the moment trying to get a lift if (we / bring) ...... a spare tyre with us.
If I (not / stay up) ...... so late last night, I (not / feel) ...... so tired this morning!
items.answer: hadn't stayed up, wouldn't be feeling

items.sentence: (I / be) ...... in terrible trouble right now if you (not / help) ...... me.
If I had a video recorder, (I / record) ...... the match last night.
(I / ask) ...... for Andy's phone number when I met him if I (not / already / have) ...... a boyfriend!
items.answer: I'd have asked, didn't already have

items.sentence: If Cody (not / live) ...... so far away, he (not / be) ...... so late to the barbecue yesterday.
Tracy (not / need) ...... extra lessons last month if she (be) ...... as good at maths as you are.
(you / get) ...... a job when you left school if the university (not / offer) ...... you a place?
items.answer: Would you have got, hadn't offered

items.sentence: If you (have) ...... as much money as she does, (you / retire) ...... by now?

sections.title: I: Inverted conditionals
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences so that the meaning remains the same.

items.sentence:
If her condition should improve, we'll inform you immediately. **Should**
Should ......, we'll inform you immediately.

Jade will ask lots of difficult questions if she gets to interview a famous politician. **Should**
Should ......, she will ask lots of difficult questions.
items.answer: Jade get to interview a famous politician

items.sentence:
If you were able to go abroad for the summer, where would you go? **Were**
Were ......, where would you go?
items.answer: you able to go abroad for the summer

items.sentence:
If I was to become a vet, I'd find putting animals down very difficult. **Were**
Were ......, I'd find putting animals down very difficult.

The world would be much better off if all environmental pollution stopped today. **Were**
Were ......, the world would be much better off.
items.answer: all environmental pollution to stop today

items.sentence:
If the hole in the ozone layer had been discovered sooner, fewer people would have got skin cancer. **Had**
Had ......, fewer people would have got skin cancer.
items.answer: the hole in the ozone layer been discovered sooner

items.sentence:
I wouldn't have become a teacher if I hadn't had such a good English teacher at school. **Had**
Had ......, I wouldn't have become a teacher.
items.answer: I not had such a good English teacher at school

sections.title: J: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: Read each line of the text. If there is an extra word, click it. If the line is correct, click OK.

items.sentence: I'm an only child. People often say to me: 'Wouldn't you be a lot ......
happier if you have had brothers and sisters?' but I don't see it like that. ......
It's true that if I had had a brother or sister, I would have someone closer ......
to my own age to talk to and play with at home, but I don't think that's ......
very important provided for you have close friends, which I do. ......
If my parents would had more children, they wouldn't be able to spend so much ......
time with me. And we have great fun together! Also, except if I had a ......
brother or sister, I'd have it to share a bedroom with them. ......
That might be fun, but what would happen if I wanted to play my CDs and he or she had ......
to study? No - I don't want a brother or sister, unless it will happens, of course. In that case, I'll think it's the best thing in the world! ......
